coredata.dc:title: Spatial confinement of MoS2 nanoparticles in jellyfish-inspired open-mouthed spheres for high-capacity and ultrafast-rate sodium-ion capture
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Xinyi
Ma, Qingtao
Wang, Luxiang
Jia, Dianzeng
Guo, Nannan
Wang, Xuemei
coredata.dc:description: Rational nanoscale structure engineering of electroactive nanoarchitecture is a very promising strategy for designing advanced capacitive deionization (CDI) materials. Herein, inspired by jellyfish physiological structure, open-mouthed MoS2/C hierarchical porous spheres were fabricated via direct-spray pyrolysis by adopting a strategy of coupling defective MoS2 with locally conductive network graphene quantum dots. Such a well-developed open-mouthed interconnected porous structure significantly stimulated the permeation of electrolyte ion the deep internal spaces of electrode, facilitated fast ion diffusion and provided abundant electrochemical adsorption sites, endowing the MoS2/C electrode with a remarkable desalination capacity of 118.83 mg g−1, an impressively rapid desalination rate of 51.35 mg g−1 min−1, and a good long-term cycle stability of 82% after 50 cycles. This work inspires the design hierarchically porous composite materials with unique micro-nanostructures, offering a new viable route for the continuous and efficient production of CDI electrode materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MoS2/C
Structure bionic design
Open-mouthed structure
Desalination capacity
Storage mechanism

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the fabrication of MoS2/CS. The SEM and TEM images of (b, f) CS, (c, g) MoS2-4/CS, (d, h) MoS2-6/CS and (e, i) MoS2-8/CS. HRTEM images of (j) CS and (k, l) MoS2-6/CS.

(a) XRD patterns of CS and MoS2/CS. (b) EPR spectra of CS and MoS2-6/CS. (c) Pore distribution and (d) pore volume of CS and MoS2/CS. High-resolution XPS spectra of (e) C 1s and (f) N 1s of CS and MoS2-6/CS. High-resolution XPS spectra of (g) Mo 3d and (h) S 2p of MoS2-6/CS. (i) Water contact angles of CS and MoS2-6/CS electrodes.

(a) The CV curves at 5 mV s−1, (b) specific capacitance of the electrodes at different scan rates and (c) GCD curves at 1 A g−1 for CS and MoS2/CS electrodes. (d) Nyquist plots of CS and MoS2/CS electrodes. (e) Cyclic stability and Coulombic efficiency of the MoS2-6/CS electrode at 5 A g−1.

(a) Dynamic SAC vs. desalination time for CS and MoS2/CS. Comparison of the properties of MoS2-6/CS electrode: (b) SAC values of various materials, (c) Ragone plot of various structures and (d) desalination performance of MoS2 composite materials. (e) SAC of MoS2-6/CS measured in various concentrations of NaCl solutions. (f) Pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order, (g) Elovich and (h) intra-particle diffusion kinetic models of CS and MoS2/CS electrodes for sorption of Na+. (i) Cycle stability of MoS2-6/CS electrode.

(a) Schematic diagram of desalination process. (b) Cross-sectional FIB-SEM image of MoS2-6/CS electrode after desalination cycle test. (c) EDS elemental line scanning spectra of C, N, O, Na, Mo and S elements. (d) XPS survey spectra and high-resolution XPS spectra of (e) Na 1s and (f) Mo 3d for MoS2-6/CS electrode in desalination test. (g) In situ Raman spectra of MoS2-6/CS electrode. (h) Power-law dependence of the response current on scan rates. (i) Capacitive and diffusion contribution of MoS2-6/CS at different scan rates.

Theoretical geometry of (a) MoS2/C and (b) Sv-MoS2/C. (c) DOS of pristine MoS2, MoS2/C and Sv-MoS2/C. (d) Charge density differences (pink and blue means accumulation and loss of electrons, respectively.) and (e) adsorption energies of Na atom at potential adsorption sites of Sv-MoS2/C. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Spatial confinement of MoS2 nanoparticles in jellyfish-inspired open-mouthed spheres for high-capacity and ultrafast-rate sodium-ion capture

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources, College of Chemistry, Xinjiang University, Urumqi, 830017, Xinjiang, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources, College of Chemistry, Xinjiang University, Urumqi, 830017, Xinjiang, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rational nanoscale structure engineering of electroactive nanoarchitecture is a very promising strategy for designing advanced capacitive deionization (CDI) materials. Herein, inspired by jellyfish physiological structure, open-mouthed MoS2/C hierarchical porous spheres were fabricated via direct-spray pyrolysis by adopting a strategy of coupling defective MoS2 with locally conductive network graphene quantum dots. Such a well-developed open-mouthed interconnected porous structure significantly stimulated the permeation of electrolyte ion the deep internal spaces of electrode, facilitated fast ion diffusion and provided abundant electrochemical adsorption sites, endowing the MoS2/C electrode with a remarkable desalination capacity of 118.83 mg g−1, an impressively rapid desalination rate of 51.35 mg g−1 min−1, and a good long-term cycle stability of 82% after 50 cycles. This work inspires the design hierarchically porous composite materials with unique micro-nanostructures, offering a new viable route for the continuous and efficient production of CDI electrode materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In response to the scarcity of fresh water, the utilization of desalination technology to extract fresh water from brackish sources has emerged as a critical approach to address the water resources crisis.[1] Capacitive deionization (CDI) has been investigated as a burgeoning desalination technology has gained significant attention due to its low energy consumption, straightforward equipment maintenance and minimal environmental impact.[2] Since Blair and Murphy published the first work on CDI-type systems in 1960, CDI technology has undergone rapid development and progressive commercialization over the past few decade.[3,4] Nevertheless, CDI technology still faces urgent challenges including limited desalination capacity and low desalination rate caused by unfavorable structures and deficient element compositions of electrode materials. The performance of CDI significantly depends on essential processes of mass transport and charge transfer at the electrochemical-confined interface.[5,6] Therefore, rationally design of advanced nanostructured electrode materials with optimally utilize the related surfaces and interface capable of providing abundant adsorption sites and facilitating rapid ion adsorption responses is imperative to improve CDI performances.

Molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) is a typical two-dimensional transition metal sulfide with adjustable physicochemical properties, rendering it a promising CDI electrode material.[7,8] However, the low electronic conductivity, severe agglomeration, and insufficient exposure of edge sites of MoS2 undesirable desalination performances.[9,10] To tackle these issues, strategies such as morphological and structural regulation, defect engineering, and integration with conductive carbon substrates are encouraging approaches. Such as, Expanding the interlayer spacing can effectively shorten the ion diffusion path and reduce transport resistance, thereby improving active sites and ion storage capacity.[11,12] Xing et al. [9] prepared ce-MoS2 nanosheets with enlarged layer spacing through chemical exfoliation, which facilitated ion intercalation/transport and achieved a desalination capacity of 8.81 mg g−1. Furthermore, the defective structures can expose more active edge sites, thus accelerating reaction kinetics and enhancing ion storage.[13–15] Additionally, incorporating a conductive carbon matrix has been demonstrated to boost electrical conductivity and help alleviate the structural instability caused by volume expansions.[16–18] Among these, top-down synthesis methods suffer from issues of low efficiency, high cost and secondary pollutants. And, bottom-up synthesis routes including chemical vapor deposition, hydrothermal and solvothermal methods constrained by complex processes and stringent reaction conditions.[19].

It is evident that an adaptable and controllable synthesis method is crucial for achieving more competitive MoS2/C composites with high desalination performance. For instance, Nguyen et al. [20] constructed a flower-like MoS2/N-doped carbon sphere composite by hydrothermal method, which significantly enhanced charge and ion transfer, resulting in an outstanding desalination capacity of 59.9 mg g−1 in a 2000 mg L−1 NaCl solution and an excellent cycling stability. Similarly, Hao et al. [16] prepared few-layer MoS2/N-doped carbon spheres via hydrothermal method, which effectively and selectively adsorbed low concentrations of Cu2+ due to their abundant active sites and defects, enlarged interlayer spacing, and improved hydrophilicity. Furthermore, Tian et al. [7] synthesized a MoS2/N-doped highly ordered mesoporous carbon composite using the hydrothermal method. This material exhibited a high desalination capacity of 28.82 mg g−1 in a 250 mg L-1 NaCl solution, attributed to the synergistic effect of electric double-layer capacitance and pseudocapacitance. However, despite the notable enhancement of reaction kinetics achieved through the integration of carbon spheres, the inner surfaces and cavities of these structures remain underutilized. As a result, MoS2 and its composites still encounter challenges of kinetic mismatch, constrained by the diffusion-controlled ion storage behavior and the extended charge transfer distance. To facilitate rapid ions and charge transfer during desalination processes, it is desirable to explore a more competitive material featuring more accessible adsorption sites, strong electron transfer, and notably shortened ion diffusion pathways to enhance the reaction kinetics of the composite materials. In addition, compared to the complexity and harsh conditions of traditional hydrothermal and solvothermal methods, ultrasonic spray pyrolysis emerges as a prominent bottom-up synthesis technology that offers greater flexibility, improved controllability, and continuous production capabilities, thereby making it a compelling choice for material synthesis.[21,22].

Herein, a unique jellyfish-like open-mouthed MoS2/hierarchical porous carbon sphere (MoS2/CS) composite was synthesized using an ultrasonic spray pyrolysis method. The morphology of MoS2/CS was controlled by tailoring the internal and external pressure difference in the droplet microreactor during pyrolysis. The strong coupling interface between the defect-rich MoS2 and the carbon skeleton of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) facilitates the charge transfer and offers abundant adsorption sites. DFT results confirmed the contribution of the coupled interface to the electron transfer. The unique nanostructure effectively enhances mass and electron transport. Especially, the open mouth in the carbon sphere accelerates the diffusion of ions into the active material. The interconnected mesoporous structure (Vmes/Vtotal: ca. 0.90) within the cavity exposes abundant edge sites, which can provide plenty of ion adsorption sites and improve desalination kinetics. Thanks to the synergistic effect of these structural and component features, the MoS2/CS electrode demonstrated a remarkable desalination capacity (118.83 mg g−1), accelerated the desalination rate (51.35 mg g−1 min−1) and improved the cycling stability (retention rate of 82% after 50 cycles). Furthermore, the obtained material also demonstrated exceptional desalination performances for various salt solutions.

The MoS2/CS samples were synthesized using ultrasonic spray pyrolysis as depicted in Fig. 1 a. The GQDs with oxygen-containing functional groups can be homogeneously dispersed in the solution forming a highly stable suspension without agglomeration. As a microreactor, droplets undergo rapid evaporation of water during the pyrolysis, which leads to the solid–liquid separation and end up with carbon spheres. Simultaneously, the H2S generated by the decomposition of thiourea reduces the MoO4 2- anions to MoS2.[23] It is worth noting that various gas phase species such as H2S, CO2, NH3 and CH4 are generated during the shock pyrolysis process.[24] These species contribute to the formation of interconnected hierarchical porous structures within the carbon sphere. Furthermore, the significant internal pressure causes a pressure disparity between the interior and exterior of the carbon sphere resulting in the bursting of the carbon shell and the formation of open cavities. The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image of CS reveals carbon spheres with smooth surfaces and an absence of discernible pore structures (Fig. 1b). During the decomposition process, the additions of various amounts of thiourea and sodium molybdate evolve into different morphologies resulting from dissimilarities in internal pressure (Fig. 1c-e). Specifically, MoS2-4/CS maintains a spherical structure with perforations on its surface. MoS2-6/CS exhibits a spherical shape with an open mouth and an interconnected hierarchical porous structure visible within the cavity at the opening. However, when the internal pressure becomes excessively high, MoS2-8/CS displays a porous and completely fragmented spherical shape. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis provides insights into the internal structure of materials (Figure f-i). The interior of CS is characterized by a solid structure without pores and enriched with highly crystallized domains corresponding to the (100) plane of GQDs (Fig. 1j). In contrast, the MoS2/CS exhibits a well-developed interconnected pore structure. Notably, the MoS2-6/CS exhibits an open cavity structure reminiscent of a jellyfish. This porous architecture, featuring open cavities, plays a crucial role in reducing the resistance of ion transport. In the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image (Fig. 1k and l), it can be seen that the lattice distances of 0.65 nm and 0.27 nm corresponding to the (002) and (100) planes of MoS2, respectively. Furthermore, the discontinuous lattice fringes, dislocations, and distortions in MoS2 demonstrate the presence of abundant defects.[14] This is due to the excess thiourea adsorbed on the surface of the primary nanocrystals, which partially hinders the directional growth of the crystals, resulting in the formation of defect-rich structure.[25] Therefore, the coupled interface between the defective MoS2 and the carbon skeleton with localized conductive networks may significantly enhance the electron transfer and provide abundant ion adsorption sites.[18,26].

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis reveals that the CS exhibits a broad reflection at approximately 24°, which corresponds to the (002) plane of graphite, indicating that the CS is predominantly composed of amorphous carbon (Fig. 2 a).[27] Notably, the XRD pattern of MoS2-4/CS displays a broad peak associated with the (002) plane of graphite, while characteristic peaks of MoS2 are absent. This absence could be attributed to the presence of ultrasmall crystallites of MoS2.[28] Furthermore, as the concentrations of molybdenum and sulfur sources in the spray precursor increase, there is an observed enhancement in both the content and crystallinity of MoS2. The MoS2/CS composite material exhibits diffraction peaks at 14.1, 33.2, 39.5 and 58.6°, which can be indexed to the (002), (100), (103) and (110) lattice planes of hexagonal MoS2, respectively (PDF#37–1492).[29,30] The phase of MoS2/CS was investigated through the utilization of Raman spectroscopy (Figure S1). The frequencies of the in-plane Mo-S phonon mode (
E
2 g
1
) and out-of-plane Mo-S mode (A1g) were identified at 377 and 408 cm−1, which aligns with the characteristics of 2H-MoS2 phase.[31,32]The peaks observed at 1340 and 1590 cm−1 are associated with the D band (vibrations of defects in sp3 carbon) and G band (graphitic sp2 hybridized carbon) of carbon materials, respectively.[33] The ID/IG value of MoS2/CS (ca. 0.95) was significantly higher than that of sample CS (0.89), indicating an increase in defects due to the introduction of pore structures. The results of electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) provide additional evidence on the presence of defects (Fig. 2b).[34] The EPR signal of MoS2-6/CS and CS is visible at g = 2.004, which is associated with the electrons trapped within sulfur vacancies. Moreover, MoS2-6/CS demonstrates a stronger signal intensity than CS, indicating a higher density of sulfur defects.

The specific surface area and pore structure of the MoS2/CS significantly influence the accessible adsorption sites and transport rates of ions. The N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of the samples are depicted in Figure S2. While CS exhibits an I-type isotherm, the MoS2/CS displays a typical IV-type isotherm with an H2-type hysteresis loop, indicating the presence of a large number of mesopores.[35] The pore size distribution reveals that micropores of about 0.50 nm dominate in CS, whereas the MoS2/CS possesses micropores of about 1.26 nm, mesopores and macropores. The macropores originate from the opening of carbon spheres (Fig. 2c). The results of the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller analysis indicate that MoS2-6/CS exhibited a higher specific surface area (757.63 m2 g−1) compared to CS (439.10 m2 g−1) (Table S1). Furthermore, it is worth noting that MoS2/CS-6 displayed a large total pore volume (0.82 cm3 g−1) and an exceptionally high mesoporous/total pore ratio of 0.89 (Fig. 2d). This ratio is considerably higher than that observed for the CS (0.21 m2 g−1 and 0.14). Compared with other hollow porous structured carbon sphere materials, this material has a significant advantage in terms of specific surface area and pore volume (Table S2). Consequently, the open interconnected hierarchical structure of MoS2-6/CS not only enhances the number of accessible adsorption sites but also acts as a rapid ion transport channel, which helps ions enter the interior of carbon spheres and promotes the transport and adsorption of ions within the carbon spheres.[35,36].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was utilized to study the surface chemistry of C, N, O, Mo and S elements in MoS2/CS, as depicted in Figure S3. The C1s spectra can be deconvolved into distinct peaks representing C-sp2 (284.8 eV), C-sp3 (285.7 eV), C-O/C-N (286.6 eV) and C=O (288.7 eV) (Fig. 2e).[34,36] The proportion of C-sp3 reflects the defects in the material, and the MoS2-6/CS exhibited a higher C-sp3 content of 22.49 % compared to the 12.19 % of CS, suggesting the presence of abundant defects (Table S3). Furthermore, the N 1s spectrum revealed the presence of pyridine N (398.7 eV), pyrrole N (400.4 eV) and graphite N (401.5 eV) (Fig. 2f),[37] and the pyridine N content in MoS2-6/CS significantly higher than that in CS. The high pyridine N content is advantageous for the adsorption of Na+ due to the preferential interaction between Na+ (hard acid) with pyridine N (hard base) through metal coordination bonds.[6] As shown in Fig. 2g, the Mo 3d spectrum of MoS2-6/CS exhibits two prominent peaks at 229.4 eV and 232.5 eV, corresponding to Mo4+ 3d5/2 and Mo4+ 3d3/2 of MoS2, respectively. In addition, a weak contribution corresponding to Mo6+ 3d3/2 appeared at 236.0 eV, indicating the presence of Mo-O-C bonds. Meanwhile, the presence of divalent sulfur 2p3/2 (162.4 eV) and S 2p1/2 (163.9 eV) in MoS2 and 2p3/2 (163.5 eV) and 2p1/2 (165.1 eV) of C-S-C derived from sulfur doping in the carbon lattice were found in the S 2p spectrum (Fig. 2h).[8,38] The FTIR spectra revealed the presence of abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on the CS surface, as evidenced by peaks corresponding to O-H, C=O, C=C and C-O stretching vibrations located at 3428 cm−1, 1700 cm−1, 1600 cm−1 and 1280 cm−1, respectively (Figure S4).[17] Additionally, MoS2/CS exhibited peaks at 1650 cm−1, 1620 cm−1 and 1370 cm−1, which are ascribed to the –NH stretching vibration, C-N stretch vibration of arenes as well as primary amides of N-H and C-N stretch vibration of amines, respectively. Furthermore, peaks associated with C=C and C-O stretching vibrations were observed near 1558 cm−1 and 1280 cm−1, while peaks appearing at 607 cm−1 and 720 cm−1 were identified as Mo-S and C-S bonds, respectively.[39]

Zeta potential measurements reveal that MoS2-6/CS (−30.32 mV) and CS (–33.66 mV) exhibit negative surface charges due to the abundant of oxygen-containing functional groups (Figure S5), which favored electrostatic adsorption of cations.[40] The dynamic contact angle test revealed that the water contact angle of the MoS2-6/CS electrode (56.3°) was significantly lower than that of CS (75.8°) (Fig. 2i and S6). Given the similar functional groups and surface charge characteristics, the significant improvement in the wettability of the MoS2/CS can be attributed to its unique open-mouthed porous structure that promotes water infiltration. This enhanced hydrophilicity is conducive to accelerated penetration of the salt solution into the electrode.

The specific capacitance and electrochemical kinetics of electrodes are the key indicators for evaluating the performance of CDI electrodes. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of CS and MoS2/CS show a quasi-rectangular shape without distinct redox peaks, exhibiting a typical double-layer capacitance (Fig. 3 a).[41] Furthermore, the MoS2-6/CS shows the largest CV curve area, suggesting that its specific capacity was significantly improved. Especially, the MoS2-6/CS electrode shows a specific capacity of 213.6 F g−1 at 1 mV s−1, and the capacity retention remains as high as 64.5% at a much faster scan rate of 50 mV s−1, demonstrating its ability to rapidly store ions (Fig. 3b). The results above are further corroborated by the symmetric triangle galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) curves, where MoS2-6/CS showed the longest discharge time (Fig. 3c). The specific capacity of the MoS2-6/CS electrode is 158.6 F g−1 at 1 A g−1, which was higher than that of CS (117.8 F g−1), MoS2-4/CS (138.8 F g−1) and MoS2-8/CS (149.4 F g−1).

The electrochemical impedance (EIS) was used to investigate and reveal the ion/electron transfer kinetics of the electrodes (Fig. 3d). In the high-frequency region, the intersection of the semicircle with the real axis indicates the equivalent series resistance (Rs), which is mainly affected by the internal and interfacial resistances of the electrode, as well as the ohmic resistance of the electrolyte. The diameter of the semicircle represents the charge transfer resistance (Rct).[29] Based on the equivalent circuit fitting results, the Rs values of MoS2-4/CS, MoS2-6/CS, MoS2-8/CS and CS are 2.40, 2.01, 1.71 and 20.6 Ω, respectively. It is evident that the favorable wettability and hierarchical porous structure of MoS2/CS significantly reduced the interfacial resistance of the electrode. Moreover, the Rct value of MoS2-6/CS (0.17 Ω) is significantly lower than that of CS (0.75 Ω), implying that the coupled interfacial structure of MoS2 and GQDs is conducive to promoting charge transfer. Furthermore, the MoS2-6/CS exhibits a near-vertical slope in the low-frequency region, suggesting robust kinetics and effective ion transfer capability.[42,43] As depicted in Fig. 3e, after 10,000 cycles at a current density of 5 A g−1, the MoS2-6/CS electrode showed an initial capacity retention rate of 99.3% and a Coulombic efficiency of 100%. The results indicate that the 3D interconnect skeleton in the open cavity endows an excellent mechanical stability and notably enhances cyclic stabilities. There are several aspects that contribute to achieving the excellent electrochemical performance. Firstly, the sulfur vacancies in MoS2 change the atomic arrangement and electronic structure, thus exposing more active sites and enhancing the electrochemical performance.[44] Furthermore, thanks to the synergistic effect of the coupled interface between the defect-rich MoS2 and the carbon skeleton with localized conductive network, it has plenty of exposed edge sites and a well-established electron transport network enabling rapid ion diffusion and electron transport. Additionally, the favorable wettability and the unique open-mouthed interconnected porous structure of MoS2-6/CS facilitate the rapid transport of ions within the internal channels.

The desalination performances of CS and MoS2/CS were evaluated in a batch mode CDI testing system at 1.2 V in a 500 mg L-1 NaCl solution. As shown in Fig. 4 a, MoS2-6/CS exhibits a maximum SAC of 54.35 mg g−1, which is significantly higher than those of CS (25.33 mg g−1), MoS2-4/CS (38.53 mg g−1) and MoS2-8/CS (47.07 mg g−1). The results demonstrate that the unique open interconnected porous structure with plentiful ion absorption sites of MoS2/CS improves the desalination performance. Compared with contemporary reported materials, the MoS2-6/CS electrode also demonstrates superior desalination capacity (Fig. 4b and S7a, Table S4). Notably, MoS2-6/CS displays a maximum desalination rate of 5.57 mg g−1 min−1, outperforming various special structures such as core–shell, nanoring and Eave-like porous carbon (Fig. 4 c).[37,42,43,47–50] The pH monitoring results showed that the pH of the solution increased from 6.2 to 6.7 during the adsorption process due to the asymmetric removal of anions and cations and the induction of the Faraday reaction, while the pH tended to decrease and reach equilibrium during desorption process (Figure S8). [45,46] This slight pH fluctuation is beneficial for the long-term desalination performance of the electrode. These findings confirm the effectiveness of the synergistic effect between the open interconnection porous structure and the strong coupling between defective MoS2 and 3D carbon frameworks in enhancing the desalination capacity and the desalination rate.

The impact of the applied voltage on the desalination ability of the MoS2-6/CS electrode was examined. The results showed that as the voltage increased, the ions diffused rapidly toward the electrode surface under the influence of the electric field. Consequently, the desalination capacity increased from 35.68 mg g−1 at 1.0 V to 57.35 mg g−1 at 1.4 V, and the desalination rate of MoS2-6/CS also sharply increased (Figure S7b). In contrast to the previously reported MoS2 composite materials, the MoS2-6/CS electrode demonstrates remarkable desalination capacity at various voltages (Fig. 4d and Table S4). Furthermore, the CDI performance of the MoS2-6/CS electrode in different concentrations of NaCl solution was investigated. As expected, the desalting capacity and rate significantly increased with the solution concentration due to the strong driving force of the concentration gradient. This enhancement resulted in a SAC of 118.83 mg g−1 and a maximum SAR of 51.35 mg g−1 min−1 in a 3000 mg L-1 NaCl solution (Fig. 4e and S9).

To evaluate the suitability of the MoS2/CS electrodes for softened and brackish water desalination applications, the desalination performance for Ca2+, Mg2+ and K+ ions was tested. The MoS2/CS electrode displayed excellent desalination capacities of 51.39 mg g−1, 63.10 mg g−1, and 55.56 mg g−1 in 5 mM KCl, CaCl2 and MgCl2 solutions, respectively (Figure S10). The analysis of the Ragone plots revealed that the MoS2/CS electrode has a high desalination rate for CaCl2, which can be attributed to the higher ionic valence, smaller hydration ion radius and hydration energy of Ca2+ (Table S5).[51] Furthermore, the desalination performance of the MoS2-6/CS electrode for brackish water was investigated. The results showed that the electrode exhibited SAC values of 79.33, 14.00, 0.58 and 3.13 mg g−1 for Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+ and K+, respectively (Figure S11a and Table S6), and still maintained a high desalination capacity after 30 cycles. Additionally, the morphology of the electrode was well maintained, demonstrating the broad applicability and promising application potential (Figure S11b and c).

To investigate the kinetics and adsorption mechanism of the electrode adsorption process, the correlation coefficients of the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetics models were determined by fitting the experimental results (Fig. 4f). In comparison, the pseudo-second-order kinetic model of the MoS2/CS electrode exhibited a higher correlation coefficient, suggesting that the adsorption process is influenced by various mechanisms, particularly the chemical adsorption process involving electron transfer (Table S7).[52] In addition, the analysis of the Elovich model revealed the occurrence of heterogeneous diffusion on the electrode surface, which is comprehensively regulated by reaction rate and diffusion factor (Fig. 4g).[53] The ion diffusion mechanism within the material was determined by fitting the intra-particle diffusion model. As depicted in Fig. 4h, the data can be segmented into three linear regions that deviate from the origin, indicating that the adsorption process is governed by external diffusion, intra-particle diffusion, and equilibrium adsorption. The first stage entails the diffusion of ions from the solution to the electrode surface, while the second stage involves the diffusion and adsorption of ions in the opened porous cavity. It is noteworthy that Kd2 is smaller than Kd1 (Table S8), which can be attributed to the rapid migration and adsorption of ions onto the MoS2-6/CS surface. Following this initial adsorption, the ions proceed to diffuse from the open pores on the MoS2-6/CS surface into the bulk phase and further diffuse through the internal interconnecting pores and adsorb at the active sites. When diffusing in the internal pores, the complexity of the pore structure and the increase in diffusion resistance result in reduction of the diffusion rate. This result indicates that the diffusion rate within the particle mainly dominates the diffusion of ions in the electrode.[54,55].

Additionally, even after undergoing 50 cycles of adsorption and desorption in a 500 mg L-1 NaCl solution, the MoS2-6/CS electrode demonstrates a remarkable cyclic stability, maintaining a capacity retention rate of up to 82% and a charge efficiency of up to 0.87 (Fig. 4i). Simultaneously, the morphology of MoS2/CS remained largely intact after cycling, and the open carbon spheres did not exhibit significant collapse (Figure S12). Therefore, favorable structural stability enables the electrode to exhibit an outstanding cycling stability.

With the aim of enabling the analysis of the ion adsorption status in MoS2-6/CS, focused ion beam scanning electron microscopy (FIB-SEM) was employed to investigate the electrode after desalination treatment (Figure S13). The cross-sectional FIB-SEM images demonstrate the presence of an interconnected hierarchical porous structure in MoS2-6/CS (Fig. 5a-b), which aligns with the TEM results. It is evident from the line scan EDS results that Na+ are adsorbed on the carbon walls of both the open-mouthed (identified by white dashed lines) and internal pores (identified by red dashed lines) (Fig. 5c). The adsorption of Na+ was further confirmed by the XPS results. As shown in Fig. 5d-e, a distinct Na 1s peak appeared during adsorption process and almost disappeared subsequent to desorption, demonstrating the reversibility of adsorption–desorption cycle during desalination process. Furthermore, the high-resolution XPS spectra of Mo 3d revealed the coexistence of both 1T and 2H phases of MoS2, suggesting that the intercalation of Na+ caused an alteration in the MoS2 structure (Fig. 5 f). The peak area corresponding to defects in C 1s decreased to 11%, indicating that the defects were involved in the adsorption of Na+ ions (Figure S14). Furthermore, in situ Raman spectroscopy further verified the adsorption and desorption process of ions in the material. As depicted in Fig. 5g, the intensity of the in-plane
E
2 g
1
peak weakened and blue-shifted during the desalination process, suggesting that sodium adsorption caused the alteration of layered structure.[56] Subsequently, during the desorption process, the peak intensity significantly increases and the peak position returned to its original position. This observation implies that the material demonstrates impressive structural efficacy in achieving rapid ion diffusion and adsorption within the internal pore network structure.

The capacitance control in the MoS2-6/CS electrode is analyzed using the power law relationship between current and scan rate. The obtained b values at various scan rates are 0.982 and 0.879 (Fig. 5h), suggesting that capacitance control primarily governs the dynamic process, which helps achieve excellent cycle stabilities and high-rate performances.[57] Furthermore, the contributions of capacitor control and diffusion control to the total capacitance are investigated. It is observed that the MoS2-6/CS electrode exhibits a high capacitance contribution rate reaching 80.2% at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1. Moreover, as the scan rate increases, the capacitance contribution rate further increases to 91.4% at 20 mV s−1, indicating that the capacitance control plays a dominant role in ion storage (Fig. 5i).

The above results demonstrate that the MoS2-coupled locally conducting carbon skeleton structure facilitates electron transfer and fast adsorption of ions in the MoS2 layered structure, as well as at edge active sites. More importantly, the open hierarchical pore characteristics not only expose abundant accessible adsorption sites, but also help to reduce the ion storage and charge transfer pathways, thus alleviating the kinetic mismatch of the material. Therefore, the carbon skeleton in MoS2-6/CS, serving as both the support and conductive network, plays a dominant role in the desalination performance of the desalination material.

The DFT calculations were performed to further elucidate the effect of the coupled interface on the electronic structure and adsorption characteristics of MoS2/CS. The geometries of MoS2/graphene (MoS2/C) and MoS2/graphene with sulfur vacancies (Sv-MoS2/C) are shown in Fig. 6 a and b, respectively. Comparative analysis of the total density of states (TDOS) reveals that pristine MoS2 displays semiconducting properties, while MoS2/C and Sv-MoS2/C exhibit enhanced conductivity attributed to electron transfer from the valence band to the conduction band (Fig. 6c). From the partial density of states (PDOS), it is evident that the contributions of the C 2p and S 3p orbitals to the conduction band increase following the introduction of sulfur vacancy, suggesting that sulfur defects are able to change the electronic structure. In addition, the increased electron density of Sv-MoS2/C at the Fermi level indicates that more current carriers are involved in electrochemical reactions at the coupling interface.[58].

The charge density difference analysis highlighted obvious electron redistribution at the coupled interface of MoS2 and graphene, and the electrons are enriched near the sulfur defects, which implies that the sulfur defects coupling interface can improve the charge transfer ability and facilitate Na+ adsorption (Fig. 6d). Consequently, Sv-MoS2/C demonstrates lower ion adsorption energies at various of potential adsorption sites (on graphene and between MoS2 and graphene, and within MoS2 layers) compared to MoS2/C, suggesting rapid Na+ storage within in the system (Fig. 6e and S15). Thus, it is evident that the coupled interface between the locally conducting network carbon skeleton of GDQs and MoS2 significantly boots charge transfer and enhances Na+ adsorption, resulting in excellent electrochemical kinetics and desalination performances.

In brief, jellyfish-like MoS2 /open-mouthed hierarchical porous carbon spheres with defect-rich MoS2 coupled localized conductive network carbon skeleton were prepared by ultrasonic spray pyrolysis. The unique open cavity and interconnected porous structure provide abundant adsorption sites, which significantly enhance ion transport. The coupling interface between defective MoS2 and carbon skeleton of the localized conducting network effectively improves the conductivity of MoS2 and exposes more accessible adsorption sites. Thanks to the aforementioned advantages, the issue of kinetic mismatch in MoS2/CS composite has been successfully mitigated. The electrode demonstrates an outstanding desalination performance (118.83 mg g−1 in 3000 mg L− 1 NaCl solution), an ultra-fast desalination rate (51.35 mg g−1 min−1) and an excellent cycle performance (retention rate of 82 % after 50 cycles in 500 mg L-1 NaCl solution). This work provides a novel approach to creating MoS2 composite carbon material with a unique open-mouthed porous structure and demonstrates its potential application in CDI.

GQDs were synthesized based on our previous work.[59,60] Typically, 6 mmol of sodium molybdate dihydrate (Na2MoO4·2H2O) and 30 mmol of thiourea (CH4N2S) were dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water and the mixture was stirred for 30 min to obtain a homogeneous solution. Subsequently, 1 g of GQDs was added to the solution under vigorous stirring, and the resulting mixture was then subjected to ultrasonic treatment for 30 min to obtain a spray precursor solution.

This spray precursor solution was injected into the ultrasonic nebulizer operating at a frequency of 1.7 MHz, with an injection speed of 0.20 ml min−1. Nitrogen gas with a flow rate of 0.45 L min−1 was used as the carrier to transport the generated aerosol into a tube furnace at 800 °C. The resulting black powder products were collected and washed several times with deionized water until the pH reached neutrality. Finally, the sample was freeze-dried to obtain the MoS2/CS sample. The products were designated as MoS2-4/CS, MoS2-6/CS, and MoS2-8/CS, which correspond to the Na2MoO4·2H2O content present in the precursor solution (4 mmol, 6 mmol and 8 mmol). Furthermore, CS refers to a sample prepared without Na2MoO4·2H2O and CH4N2S in the precursor solution.

Xinyi Gong: Writing – original draft, Data curation, Conceptualization. Qingtao Ma: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Methodology. Luxiang Wang: Supervision, Resources, Project administration. Dianzeng Jia: Supervision, Resources, Investigation. Nannan Guo: Software, Methodology, Formal analysis. Xuemei Wang: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
